document.title: Notes Towards a Reducer Core Document
document.purpose: Collect architectural notes, distinctions, questions, terminology, boundaries, candidate outlines, and conceptual discoveries towards a future formal Reducer Core document.
author: Lion Kimbro
Wing-Cat (Chat-GPT)

orientation.summary: This document is not itself the Reducer Core document. It is a staging ground for discoveries, distinctions, architectural principles, unresolved questions, candidate outlines, terminology work, and implementation-oriented thinking related to the Reducer Core architecture.
orientation.goal: Stabilize the conceptual architecture sufficiently that future implementations become more predictable, disciplined, and consciously structured.
orientation.important-note: The purpose is discovery and organization, not premature canonization.

core-discovery.summary: Reducer Core and Blackboard-Judge appear to operate on fundamentally different temporal models.
working-formulation: Blackboard-Judge asks: 'What is happening right now?'
core-discovery.importance: This distinction appears foundational and may become one of the central organizing principles of the eventual architecture.

important-architectural-claim.claim: Reducer events are normalized semantic events, not raw interaction phenomena.

many-inputs-one-semantic-event.interaction: User performs a mouse gesture recognized as quit

important-architectural-claim.important-consequence: Reducer Core becomes independent of the interaction modality that produced the semantic event.

possible-central-principles: Reducer Core consumes semantic events, not raw interaction events.
Not every event deserves semantic historicity.
Reducer state is orientation within reality, not reality itself.
Reducer Core lives in discrete semantic transitions.
Interaction systems recognize meaning; reducers process meaning.
Reducer Core should not become a dumping ground for all state.

semantic-events: What qualifies as a semantic event?
Relationship between semantic events and history.
relationship-to-world-model: Reducer references world state without containing all world data.
relationship-to-blackboard-judge: Why reducers should not directly consume raw interaction streams.

possible-misunderstandings-to-guard-agai: Reducer Core is not merely a place where state changes happen.
Reducer Core is not identical to Redux.
Reducer Core is not the entire application state.
Not every GUI occurrence should become a reducer event.
Reducer Core should not directly process raw pointer movement.
Interaction interpretation should not be collapsed into reducer logic.
Projection should not own semantic truth.
candidate-formulations: Reducer Core is a constitutional engine for semantic transition.
Reducer Core processes normalized semantic declarations.
Reducer Core converts semantic events into lawful session/world transitions.
Reducer Core coordinates workspace orientation within larger reality.
possible-questions: What makes an event semantically meaningful?
Should selection belong to reducer state?
Should reducers emit world mutation commands or world mutation events?
How should reducer history interact with world history?
How much reducer state should be persistent?
What belongs in workspace state versus world state?
Should reducers generate intended world revision IDs?

section-4.title: Reducer State
possible-contents: What should and should not live in reducer state.

merchant-contact-database.importance: Potentially ideal first canonical reducer-core implementation.
reasoning: Good for studying reducer event design.
Good for studying history and persistence.

possible-slogans: Reducer Core lives in semantic transition.
Reducer state is orientation within reality.
Interaction recognizes meaning; reducers process meaning.
Reducer Core consumes semantic declarations, not raw phenomena.
notes: This document should remain flexible and exploratory.
Architectural discoveries should be preserved before cleanup.
Implementation experience should guide later canonization.

evening.interaction #1:
Reducer Core NOT about state mutation.
  It's about:
    - authoritative reality reconstruction from event streams

  * a Lawful Center
  * granted Authority
  * Receives Claims/Events/Messages/Intents
  * Reconstruction from Event Stream

  Continuous/Interaction Sensing -- Raw/Tokenizer/Judge/Organism
  Reducer Core -- Discrete Events to Authoritative Editor State
  Projection -- Presentation to the User
  World State -- Records of the Larger World

  Events vs. Commands vs. Facts?

  What events enter the Reducer:
  - User driven events, interpreted by mechanisms
    - tkinter widget based events
    - events interpreted by organisms (Blackboard-Judge system)

  Which things emerge from the Reducer:
  - changes to the World State
  - updates to the tkinter system state

  What kind of state the Reducer Core owns:
  - the state of the editing environment
  - the index into world states that is active presently

  Does the reducer emit secondary effects?
  ( I'm not sure what that means. )

  How Time Works
  - I wonder if the Raw/facts layer should be visible to the reducer core?
    Or should it have nothing that depends on, say for instance, the current time?
    - Or should the present time be passed to it as an event?

  Are Reducers Pure?
  - I say Yes.
  - Time signals are events.

  Do Reducers replay from logs?
  - When running tests, you are doing basically that.

  How do Reducers compose?  Subreducers?  Hierarchy of reducers?
  - Assumption: This means reducers with reducers inside.
    - I suppose it can be done, but this isn't a priority right now.

  Can Reducers eject events?
  - NO.

  What does "official reliaty" mean?
  - Put that bong away; We're not concerned with this jibber jabber.
  - Bring an actual problem, or get out of the engineering hall.

  
  What problem is the reducer-core fundamentally solving?

  My stream of consciousness essay:
    
    - It makes it possible to separate all of the gui nonsense, from what the app actually does.
      - Thus reducing errors, dramatically.
      - Making it far easier to reason over the program.

    So the problem it solves, very practically stated,
    is the spaghetti code problem of GUI code mixing in with
    how the program actually works, at an abstract level.

    Therefore, making it easier to write complex applications.

    In particular, editors, which are near and dear to my heart.


  We need to keep the insight that Blackboard/Judge is fundamentally continuous, and the Reducer Core is fundamentally punctuated.

  Blackboard-Judge is fundamentally: situated embodied cognition.
  Reducer-Core is fundamentally: Semantic historical law.

  Wing-Cat said it well:

Blackboard-Judge	Reducer-Core
continuous	discrete
episode-based	transition-based
embodied	semantic
perceptual	declarative
coordination	commitment
ongoing	punctuated
interaction ecology	historical record
“what is happening?”	“what happened?”
temporal negotiation	lawful transition

  Basic Map

CONTINUOUS REALITY
(raw embodied interaction)
        ↓
BLACKBOARD-JUDGE
(recognize ongoing situations)
        ↓
SEMANTIC EVENTS
(discrete meaning extraction)
        ↓
REDUCER-CORE
(authoritative semantic transitions)
        ↓
WORLD MODEL
(durable modeled reality)
        ↓
PROJECTION
(visible manifestation)


  Yes, the document should define: What qualifies as a reducer event.
  "Not every occurrence deserves semantic historicity."


  I think I want something like:

- a map of core architecture:
  - blackboard-judge
  - reducer-core
  - world-model
  - projection

- additional maps
  - "Maps" are depictions of the relationships between things.
  - they don't need names more sophisticated than "Core Architecture Map"
  - or "Map of Flows within and Around the Reducer Core"

- a dictionary of concepts
  - a brief on them
  - essential elements in detail
  - pointers to related concepts
  - questions about the concept, and their basic answers

  So some of the items will be like:

* "Event" (Reducer Core)
  -- where we talk about what is an event?
  -- good candidates/bad candidates

You said:
---
What’s missing now is:

constitutional naming
divisions of authority
event taxonomy
lawful boundaries
canonical examples
---

  I think that's pretty much what we need in this dictionary.
evening.interaction #2:
A few notes:

#1 -- I think we should put together a collection of maps and dictionary entries, then.

  Map -- the four major divisions:
  1. blackboard/judge (we need a better name for this -- maybe "continuity engagement")
  2. reducer-core (maybe also needs a better name? "discrete symbolic core"?)
  3. world-model
  4. projection

  Each of these is an individual "concept", too, for the dictionary,
  but the treatment as a map is also important.
  And the map can also point to the event stream feeding into the reducer-core from
  the GUI environment, and from the "continuity engagement system" (or whatever we call it)


#2 -- Yes, the documentation should NOT resolve everything; just lay out the general lay of the land.

  Every program is different, and typically does things a little bit differently.
  As long as we preserve architectural insight, I think we can figure out what to do.

  But there are common features I need -- things like do/undo, and doing it right can be tricky.
  I want good bedding here, so that Claude Code or Codex will know what I'm looking for,
  and how to implement it well.


#3 -- I like Maps, Dictionary, Skeletons, Case Studies.

  But I think we won't have Case studies at first, and maybe just barely skeletons.
  Let's start with Maps and Dictionaries, and stub the skeletons and case studies sections.
evening.interaction #3:
Before we write the first version of the maps and concepts dictionary, --

Suggest names to replace blackboard-judge and to replace reducer-core.


And for the maps, I think the first map will look something like:

----

I. Continuity Engine (aka "Blackboard Judge")

  Raw Facts Collection
    |
    V
  Tokenizers
    |
    V
  Judge & Organisms

  Organisms can emit:
  --> Events (for Discrete Engine)
  --> Immediates (for Presentation)

  DOCUMENTED PRIMARILY IN BLACKBOARD JUDGE DOCUMENTATION


II. Discrete Engine (aka "Reducer Core")

  Processes Events,
  Changes State,
  Emits Effects

  --> World State Changes
  --> User Interface Effects (update signals)

  DOCUMENTED PRIMARILY AS "REDUCER CORE" SYSTEM

  Do/Undo is primarily implemented here,
  and represnts going back to prior state.

  State is intentionally (relatively) small,
  and represents the state of the editor,
  including text buffer contents and such.


III. World Model

  Essentially represents encyclopedic data storage.

  Has a Do/Undo System quite different than the Discrete Engine's.

  The Discrete Engine keeps an index key into a state of the world model.
  When changes are applied, a new index key is supplied as well,
  essentially a GUID to associate with the new state, and to be able
  to recall (if possible.)


IV. Projection

  Projection has two primary aspects:

  1. GUI User Interface Alignment
    - the GUI shall be updated to reflect the Editor State (per the Discrete Engine)

  2. Canvas/Graphical Display Alignment
    - the representation of the World Model as depicted on the tkinter.Canvas
      (or whatever immediate or retained mode representations exist)
      are updated (or projected) to match the current World Model state
    - any immediate effects are also represented on the display
      (for example, a selection marque being drawn frame by frame)

----

As for the layout of the dictionary entries:

* identifier
* title
* concise meaning
* why it exists
* what it owns
* what it does NOT own
* neighboring concepts
* examples
* anti-examples
* open questions

Yes.
